source_file: A Spore-Forming Probiotic Supplement Improves the Intestinal Immune Response and Protects the Intestinal Health During Recurrent Clostridioides difficile Colonization in Mice.txt

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic supplementation reduces inflammation by decreasing expression of TNFα and calprotectin.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Lower expressions of TNFα and calprotectin (P ≤ .05) were observed in CD-PRO mice compared with CD-S mice.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic supplementation supports intestinal barrier integrity by enhancing expression of tight junction proteins.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: The probiotics also supported the expression of intestinal tight junction proteins, which were diminished in the proximal colon of CD-S mice (P < .05).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic supplementation modulates nutrition immunity by lowering serum iron levels.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Probiotic-supplemented mice had increased messenger RNA expression of several immune parameters, accompanied by lower serum iron levels compared with CD-S mice (P < .05).

key_findings: Probiotic supplementation mitigated antibiotic-induced changes in cecum weight (P < .001).
Probiotic-supplemented mice had increased mRNA expression of several immune parameters and lower serum iron levels compared with CD-S mice (P < .05).
Lower expressions of TNFα and calprotectin (P ≤ .05) were observed in CD-PRO mice compared with CD-S.
Probiotics supported the expression of intestinal tight junction proteins, which were diminished in the proximal colon of CD-S mice (P < .05).
relevance_to_research_topic: This paper does not directly study high-iron diets, but it reports that serum iron levels are linked to the severity of recurrent Clostridioides difficile colonization and that modulation of iron (through probiotic supplementation) affects immune responses and intestinal barrier function. This suggests a role for iron metabolism in CDI pathophysiology and supports the relevance of investigating 'diet–gut microbiota–host' interactions, including iron, in CDI susceptibility and disease severity.
